text: {"claims":[{"statement":"In a randomised controlled trial with 23 participants with metabolic syndrome, consuming three eggs per day for four weeks increased plasma antioxidants compared with baseline and a choline supplement.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"In the final RCT in 23 participants with MetS, 3 eggs/day or a choline supplement was given for 4 weeks. Plasma antioxidants significantly increased during the egg intervention compared with baseline and choline.","entailment":"The quoted sentence reports that plasma antioxidants rose significantly during the egg intervention relative to baseline and the choline supplement, directly supporting the claim.","population":"participants with MetS","intervention":"3 eggs/day for 4 weeks","comparator":"baseline and choline supplement","outcome":"plasma antioxidants increased","timeframe":"4 weeks","quantifiers":"","modality":"causal"},{"statement":"In the same trial, plasma choline increased during both the egg and choline interventions, but plasma trimethylamine N‑oxide did not increase during either treatment.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"Plasma choline significantly increased during both interventions but there were no concurrent increases in plasma trimethylamine _N_-Oxide (TMAO) during either treatment.","entailment":"The quote states that plasma choline rose in both groups while TMAO levels did not change, supporting the claim.","population":"participants with MetS","intervention":"egg consumption and choline supplement","comparator":"","outcome":"plasma choline increased; plasma TMAO did not increase","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"causal"},{"statement":"Observational studies and randomised controlled trials give conflicting evidence on whether egg consumption raises or lowers the risk of type 2 diabetes.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"observational and RCT findings directly conflict on the issue of whether eating eggs raises or lowers the risk of T2D.","entailment":"The quoted sentence explicitly says that observational and RCT findings conflict regarding the effect of eggs on T2D risk, which underpins the claim.","population":"","intervention":"egg consumption","comparator":"","outcome":"risk of T2D","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"associational"},{"statement":"It is implausible that eggs play a causal role in the development of type 2 diabetes.","claimType":"value","quote":"Hence, it is implausible that eggs play a causal role in the development of T2D and there is evidence that nutrients or bioactives in the yolk may have a positive impact on metabolic markers.","entailment":"The quote expresses the author's judgment that eggs are unlikely to causally cause T2D, providing the basis for the claim.","population":"","intervention":"egg consumption","comparator":"","outcome":"development of T2D","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"normative"},{"statement":"In a 3‑month energy‑restricted diet study with 128 adults at risk of or with type 2 diabetes, high egg intake did not lead to greater weight loss compared with a low egg diet.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"No significant differences in weight loss between high egg vs. low egg diets.","entailment":"The quoted result directly states that weight loss was not different between the high‑egg and low‑egg groups, supporting the claim.","population":"128 adults at risk of or with T2D","intervention":"high egg diet (12/week) for 3 months","comparator":"low egg diet (<2/week)","outcome":"weight loss","timeframe":"3 months","quantifiers":"","modality":"causal"},{"statement":"Egg breakfast compared with bagel breakfast reduced energy intake at lunch by 112 kcal and over 24 hours by 403 kcal in a crossover trial of 21 men.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"EI at lunch (−112 kcal) and over 24 h (−403 kcal) significantly lower after eggs Vs. bagel.","entailment":"The quote provides the quantitative energy intake reductions after egg breakfast versus bagel, confirming the claim.","population":"21 men (20–70 y)","intervention":"egg breakfast","comparator":"bagel breakfast","outcome":"energy intake at lunch and over 24 h","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"−112 kcal at lunch; −403 kcal over 24 h","modality":"causal"},{"statement":"Egg breakfast increased satiety and reduced hunger compared with cereal or croissant breakfasts in a crossover trial of 30 men.","claimType":"empirical","quote":"After eggs, increased satiety, less hunger and lower desire to eat vs. cereal or croissant.","entailment":"The quoted sentence reports higher satiety and lower hunger after egg breakfast relative to cereal or croissant, supporting the claim.","population":"30 men (21.7 ± 1.2 y)","intervention":"egg breakfast","comparator":"cereal breakfast; croissant breakfast","outcome":"satiety increased; hunger decreased","timeframe":"","quantifiers":"","modality":"causal"}]}
